title: ObjectBox (Flutter Object DB)
objectives: Understand ObjectBox via a real-life analogy
See its object-oriented model + built-in vector search

sections.markdown:
**Think of ObjectBox as an object storage room where you put entire toys instead of parts on shelves.**

- In a **relational DB (SQL)**, you break every toy down into parts and store them on labeled shelves (columns) — the toy's wheels on one shelf, its body on another. Reassembling on read costs work.
- In **ObjectBox**, you store the WHOLE toy as-is. When you retrieve it, you get the object back exactly as you put it in. No decomposition, no reassembly, no ORM mapping layer.

**Concretely:** ObjectBox is a NoSQL, object-oriented database for Flutter, Android, iOS, and desktop. **You store Dart objects directly** — no ORM ceremony, no manual JSON serialization. Fast (their benchmarks put it faster than SQLite/Realm/Hive for most operations). Backed by native C++ code + Dart FFI.

**Where it shines:**
- **Object-graph-heavy apps** — deep relations (User → Orders → OrderItems → Products) navigate naturally.
- **On-device AI + vector search** — built-in HNSW vector search for RAG / semantic search / embeddings (a rare feature for embedded DBs).
- **Cross-platform sync** — ObjectBox Sync (commercial) provides multi-device sync out of the box.
- **High-throughput workloads** — real-time analytics, sensor logging, chat.

**Trade-offs:**
- **Smaller community** than SQLite/Hive/Isar in the Flutter world.
- **Sync is a paid feature** (ObjectBox Sync) — not free.
- **Native code + FFI** — larger app size than pure-Dart Hive/Isar.
- **Licensing** — Apache 2.0 for core, commercial for Sync + Vector Search (as of 2026, verify current terms).

**When to consider ObjectBox:** you specifically need on-device vector search for AI/RAG use cases, or you're already using ObjectBox in the Android/Java world and want Dart parity. Otherwise, Drift / Isar / Hive cover most Flutter needs first.

**ObjectBox** is a NoSQL, object-oriented database for mobile + embedded, with native Dart / Flutter bindings. Data lives in **boxes** of typed Dart objects. Uses a native C++ core via FFI for speed. Supports **ToOne / ToMany relations**, **reactive queries**, **encryption**, and (as of ObjectBox 4+) **built-in HNSW vector search** for embeddings — a differentiator vs Hive/Isar. Multi-platform sync available (paid). From the German company ObjectBox Ltd. Best for object-graph-heavy or vector-search Flutter apps.
## Sample program — ObjectBox in Flutter
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Not closing queries.** Every `.build()` opens a native resource; leaks pile up. Always `.close()`. Or use `Query.findAsync()` which closes automatically.
**Mistake 2: Forgetting to persist relations.** `user.orders.add(order)` only updates the in-memory link; you need `userBox.put(user)` (or `applyToDb: true` param on some methods) to persist.
**Mistake 3: Not indexing frequently-queried fields.** Same rule as any DB — `@Index` on WHERE columns.
**Mistake 4: Assuming sync is free.** ObjectBox Sync is a paid product; verify pricing before committing to it as your sync solution.
**Mistake 5: Ignoring the `objectbox_flutter_libs` size hit.** Native libs add MBs to your app; use split-per-abi releases (`--split-per-abi` in Flutter build).
## Best practices
**1. Always close queries.** Or use `findAsync`.
**2. `@Index` + `@Unique`** on business identifiers (email, sku).
**3. ToOne / ToMany for relations** — objects link naturally.
**4. Batch writes in a transaction** — `store.runInTransaction()`.
**5. Vector search — leverage HNSW for on-device RAG.** ObjectBox's differentiator.
**6. Backup by copying the ObjectBox directory** — files, easy.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — User + Order entities.** Put, get, query by email.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Relations.** ToMany from User to Orders; iterate.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Reactive query.** `.watch()` bound to StreamBuilder.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Vector search.** Compute embeddings for 100 documents; find top-5 similar to a query embedding.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Batched writes.** Insert 100k users in a single transaction; measure vs one-at-a-time.
## Interview Q&A

**Q1: What is ObjectBox?**

**A:** ObjectBox is a NoSQL, object-oriented database for Flutter + Dart (also Android, iOS, desktop). You store Dart objects directly — no ORM mapping layer. Native C++ core via FFI for speed. Supports ToOne/ToMany relations, reactive queries, encryption, and (as of ObjectBox 4+) built-in **HNSW vector search** for on-device AI / semantic search — a differentiator among embedded DBs. **Best for:** object-graph-heavy Flutter apps, apps needing on-device vector search, teams already using ObjectBox on Android. **Not for:** most Flutter apps — Drift, Isar, or Hive cover typical needs and have larger communities.

**Q2: ObjectBox vs Isar for a Flutter app?**

**A:** Both are NoSQL, fast, cross-platform, native-backed. **ObjectBox** — built-in vector search (huge for RAG/AI features), commercial sync product, longer history (originated in Android/Java world). **Isar** — Flutter-first design, pure Rust, active in the Flutter ecosystem, from the Hive author. **Rule for 2026:** if you specifically need on-device vector search or paid sync → ObjectBox. Otherwise → Isar has the momentum in Flutter. Both are fast and capable; the choice comes down to specific features and team familiarity. Neither is dramatically better for typical CRUD workloads.

**Q3: How do relations work in ObjectBox?**

**A:** Two types: **`ToOne<T>`** — 1:1 relation. `order.owner.target = user;` establishes the link. **`ToMany<T>`** — 1:N or N:M. `user.orders.add(order);`. **Bidirectional** — ObjectBox generates the back-references automatically. **Lazy loading** — accessing `user.orders` triggers a DB query for related items. **Persistence** — after modifying a relation, call `box.put(user)` to save. **Compared to SQL FKs:** no separate join table needed for many-to-many; ObjectBox handles the mapping internally. **Compared to Isar's IsarLinks:** ObjectBox integrates a bit more seamlessly (no explicit `.load()` needed for most cases); Isar's model is more explicit.

**Q4: What is HNSW and why is it valuable in a DB?**

**A:** **HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)** is a graph-based nearest-neighbor search algorithm — the industry standard for vector similarity search. Given a query vector, HNSW returns the top-K most similar vectors in near-logarithmic time, even across millions of vectors. **Why in ObjectBox:** RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) and on-device semantic search need this. Traditionally you'd run a separate vector DB (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma). ObjectBox has it BUILT IN — one library covers both structured data + vectors. For Flutter apps building on-device AI features (search, chatbots, semantic content), this is a big deal. **Trade-off:** the vector-search feature may be gated (verify current licensing).

## Real-world usage

- **Android/Java apps** — ObjectBox has been popular in native Android for years; Flutter bindings are the newer path.
- **On-device AI apps** — the vector search feature is unique among embedded DBs.
- **Multi-device apps** — ObjectBox Sync (paid) provides sync.
- **Not commonly seen** in typical Flutter apps yet — Drift/Isar dominate that space.
- **When to consider:** genuinely needing HNSW, or already in an ObjectBox shop.

## Advanced notes

- **`Store.runInTransaction()`** — batch writes.
- **Async queries** — `.findAsync()` runs off UI thread.
- **Query builder** — supports `and`, `or`, `not`, ordering, limit, offset.
- **Encryption** — enable with `openStore(macosApplicationGroup: ..., debugFlag: DebugFlags.logAsyncQueue)` and specify encryption key.
- **`admin`** — ObjectBox Admin lets you inspect the DB in a browser at dev time.
- **Backup** — copy the ObjectBox directory (data.mdb, lock.mdb files).
- **Sync** — ObjectBox Sync product supports multi-device, offline-first, conflict resolution.
- **App size** — native libs add ~5-10 MB; use `--split-per-abi` when building.
- **Type support** — primitives, strings, DateTime, List<primitives>, custom @Property(type:) mappings.
- **Migrations** — schema evolution via UID annotations; complex migrations may need manual data copy.

flashcards.back: NoSQL object DB; native C++ core via FFI; ToOne/ToMany relations; built-in HNSW vector search (4+).
Built-in HNSW vector search — for on-device AI/RAG use cases.
ToOne<T> for 1:1, ToMany<T> for 1:N. Bidirectional. Persist with box.put().
Fluent DSL — .query(X_.field.equals(y)).build().find(). Always .close() or use findAsync.
ObjectBox Sync = paid product. Not part of open-source core.
Need on-device vector search or already an ObjectBox shop. Otherwise Isar/Drift/Hive.

options: ToOne<T> and ToMany<T> with automatic back-refs
